metadata.name: Copper
metadata.title: Copper Settings
metadata.description: When laser cleaning copper, we've found that starting with gentle pulses works best because its shiny surface bounces back most of the energy, so you get even coverage without wasting shots. This high reflectivity sets it apart from duller metals like iron, meaning we adjust our setup to focus on short, repeated passes that gradually lift dirt and oxides while the metal's strong heat-spreading ability prevents hot spots. But watch out midway—push too hard and the surface can soften unevenly, so we dial back intensity and overlap scans to keep things cool and restore that clean, conductive finish for electronics or plumbing jobs.

author.name: Todd Dunning
author.title: MA
credentials: MA Optics and Photonics, UC Irvine, 2019
author.imageAlt: Todd Dunning, MA, Junior Optical Materials Specialist at Coherent Inc., with precision optics tools

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Copper oxide removal and surface cleaning

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Copper absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Optimal spot diameter for balancing cleaning efficiency and processing speed

repetitionRate.description: High repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage while maintaining process control

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Copper oxide removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective oxide removal without excessive thermal diffusion

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning with adequate energy deposition

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal pulse overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation

power.description: Typical range for industrial 1064 nm fiber lasers cleaning copper; start at 200 W to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage due to copper's high reflectivity and conductivity. Max safe before substrate melting ~500 W with proper scanning. Source: IPG Photonics specs and study in Journal of Laser Applications (2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contamination removal; 2000 mm/s recommended for light contaminants on copper to minimize heat accumulation. Slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy oxidation, faster for surface prep. Source: CleanLaser equipment manuals and Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime typical for copper cleaning at 1064 nm; 50 ns recommended for minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ) while ablating contaminants without melting substrate. Picosecond (ps) options (1-10 ps) reduce HAZ further but increase cost. Femtosecond less common industrially. Source: Laser physics review in Applied Surface Science (2019).

repetition_rate.description: Standard for pulsed cleaning; 50 kHz balances speed and heat management on reflective copper—higher rates (>80 kHz) accelerate cleaning but risk accumulation. Lower for heavy contaminants. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2017) on metal ablation.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe contaminant removal; copper damage threshold ~4-5 J/cm² at 1064 nm, so 1.5 J/cm² recommended to ablate oxides without substrate harm, considering high thermal conductivity. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Physical Review B (2015) and Journal of Applied Physics (2021).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for industrial cleaning; 200 μm recommended for copper to achieve uniform fluence on detailed surfaces vs. larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. Smaller for precision. Source: Laser equipment specs from Coherent Inc. and process studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (2019).

pass_count.description: Typically 1-3 passes for complete removal on copper; 2 passes recommended for multi-layer contaminants to ensure uniformity without excessive heat. Single pass for light cleaning. Source: Industry case studies in Laser Cleaning Handbook (2022) and practical reports from SPIE proceedings.

overlap_ratio.description: Standard for uniform coverage; 60% recommended for copper to prevent gaps in cleaning while avoiding overexposure and thermal stress. Higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination, lower for speed. Source: Process optimization in Journal of Cleaner Production (2020).
